channel.title: Arcanum on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Arcanum on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Fallout vs. Arcanum Story States
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout versus Arcanum’s story states.

This is something I’ve mentioned many, many times on here. I always talk about Fallout having three and Arcanum having 27. Most recently, Uh, I did it in a video and Richard to the Argo asked: “Hey Tim, can you do a video breaking down what a 27 act story looks like? You’ve mentioned it a few times now and I can’t quite wrap my head around that number.

item.title: Resurrecting The Arcanum IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about resurrecting the Arcanum intellectual property.

I get asked a lot on this channel in the comment section about making more Arcanum. And I have videos on doing a remake of Arcanum, You know, bringing it up to modern standards — hardware code, UI. There’s a video on.

I’ll link it below. I’ve also talked about remastering Arcanum. I’ve also talked about which.

item.title: Arcanum Experience Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Arcanum experience point system, the design behind it and how it’s kind of contentious.

It was to the dev team back in the day and players today still talk about it. So let’s dive into it. This actually comes from a question from blah vlah, who asked: “Can you talk about the experience system in Arcanum? I’m not sure there’s another game with similar displacement of XP, being mostly on a per hit basis, with a pretty minor emphasis on the per kill point. This makes most solo builds very strong, but politician characters that collect a lot of followers or mages that specialize in summoning get very little experience. What was your design philosophy here? It’s a good question.

item.title: Non-Expository Arcanum Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about non-expository Arcanum lore.

This is a companion video to the non-expository Fallout lore that I did a while back, where Fath Daniel asked: “What is Arcanum, non-expository lore” And I thought, “Yeah, I should do that because that makes sense” And I It’s something that it’s interesting. Um, it was harder for me to do this for Arcanum than for Fallout And because Fallout was a smaller game in sense of scope, number of NPCs, number of dialogues, cutscenes, in-game books — So it was harder for me to find Arcanum lore that had never been, that had never appeared in the game already. Like, there’s a cut scene that mentions the lore or an NPC talks about it at length, and we had several NPCs that could talk about a lot of lore that you may not know, because it was hidden behind a, speech skill, or you had to have a certain reactivity level with them, or you just never dove down. In their dialogue tree to find it. And then there were lots and lots and lots of books. So I tried to find lore for Arcanum.

item.title: Arcanum Magick Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum magic design.

But first let me address what’s going on. New digs haven’t had time to even unpack the books into the bookshelves when Anubis1101 asked some really good questions And, as a super mutant, he gets a priority video, which means I just dropped what I was doing. I said “Stop Hammer time”, well, stop Arcanum time And did this video And it’ll be up. Um, I think Monday’s is already up, so it’ll be up on Wednesday. But then expect things to go back to the old digs for a while, because I had a lot of video in the queue. But anyway, let’s get into Anubis’s questions. He asked: “Tim, I’m really curious about the magic colleges in Arcanum and how you would approach such a system for a new game.

item.title: Arcanum Fate Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum Fate Points.

Yes, I want to talk about a very particular feature in a specific game that’s almost 25 years old. But this comes from a question from Beefy NFN. I know what that means. Wait, no, I don’t. I have no idea.

Let’s continue. Asked: “I’d really like to hear you go into detail about how you balanced Arcanum with fate points. As someone who has never seen gameplay of Arcanum, let alone played it, The idea of fate points is kind of offputting to me as a game mechanic. As a player, I tend to hoard items that are one use and only use them when absolutely necessary, which means I end the game with a bunch of set items. So I’m curious how that applies in Arcanum with Fate Points.

item.title: Keeping Engine & Game Separate In Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about keeping the game and the engine separate in Arcanum.

This comes from a question from All Lucky 7 who asked, “Interesting, that the mechanics and the engine part of the game were kept separate. Would you mind explaining how that is or was done”. So if you’ve watched this channel and you know I’ve referenced TIG and Gnol — my operating system, abstraction libraries, a ton. There’s a video I’ll link below.

item.title: Challenges Facing An Arcanum Remaster
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about challenges facing an, Arcanum remaster.

I did a video like this — challenges facing a Fallout remaster — couple months ago. It was popular, there was a lot of comments and many of them said, “Hey, I’d rather have an Arcanum remaster” And I thought: well, I made an Arcanum remake video, which I’ll also link below. But this is a remaster. This is about: hey, let’s take the original code and just fix bugs and make it work on modern hardware and get it optimized, not let’s make a 3D version of Arcanum with lasers H, let me write down lasers. Okay, this video is probably going to be kind of long because whoever chooses to do this remaster of Arcanum, you got your work cut out for you. Also, it’s long because I can be very, very specific. I own the code. I’ve looked at the code. I looked at the code for a few hours before making this video because I wanted to make sure.

item.title: Arcanum Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. I want to talk to you today about Arcanum lore exploration.

What I mean by that is kind of a deep dive into some of the Arcanum lore that interests me. Now I did a video already on the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum, and there’s a video about it. I’ll link it below, and in that there was this idea for combining magic and tech. There was an ore that let you make Tech ological items that you could magically imbue, and I didn’t like it then. And now, 25 years later, I absolutely hate the idea, and the reason for that is I think it takes a fundamental pillar of the Arcanum setting, which is Magic and Tech are diametrically opposed, not because they don’t like each other, not because they don’t get along and you need to find, you know, a substrate to get them, you know, happy to be together because they work diametrically oppositely.

item.title: Arcanum Backgrounds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about Arcanum backgrounds and I will talk a little bit about them.

I want to give you some context, but then I will answer this question from Feliciano frontado, 3134, who asked: fun Friday suggestion: what were your favorite backgrounds in Arcanum? This is fun to go look at because I loved backgrounds in Arcanum and in fact I kind of want give you a little background on backgrounds. See what I did there. It wasn’t funny.

item.title: Editors Day Talk, April 2000
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to try to recreate the presentation I gave at editor’s day in April of 2000.

I have a video about it. I’ll link below. We did an Arcanum press event at the Magic Castle in April of 2000, about a year before Arcanum came out.

I found my notes on this. I thought they were lost, but I think I’ve told you I have so many paper backups, so many hard drive backups in different formats, that I finally found my notes.

item.title: Arcanum Optimization
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum optimization.

This comes from a set of questions from uh, a viewer, diiz, who asks a few topic suggestions for upcoming videos: how to optimize game code, how to even find what is wise to optimize, and different ways to measure results of optimization, and a bunch more questions too that didn’t have to do with optimization. So so I’m going to stop it right there. I may get the other ones later, but let me just talk about optimization, and I’ve decided to go back to Arcanum because I can talk about that code, I own that code, I can look at that code, I looked at that code and um it. Lets me just be much more specific about what I’m going to talk about now. I already have a general optimization video, which I’ll link below, which talks about basic techniques for code optimization, like when to do them, uh, when sometimes changing the design specification can lead to faster code or code that uses less memory. So sometimes the optimization is not in code, it’s change your design, even just a little bit, and you can end up with way better code. I also have a video on the specific memory savings that we got in Arcanum through object compaction. I will summarize it again here, but I recommend watching that video too.

item.title: Solo vs. Party
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Solo versus party character control.

This is something that was asked most recently. I think it’s been asked several times, but it asked most recently by Tom FS, who asked: I was wondering if you could go over your thoughts on full party control versus only controlling your character. I view this separately from the full party creation versus individual character creation debate. What’s the lack of party control in Arcanum due to multiplayer? I know it was that case for never winner nights.

item.title: Arcanum "Dat" Files
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s dat files.

Dat yesterday’s video was all about all the data that Arcanum had. I was talking about how much. We had a lot of data and we had to store it, and if you’ve ever tried to mess around in the Arcanum folders, you’re noticed that the data, most of the data — is kept in big monolithic files whose extension is dat for data. I want to talk about real quickly how we did that and then why we did that. Um, because it makes sense, if you’re just curious about making a game, or if you’re curious about modding a game or patching a game, because there’s a whole bunch of reasons.

item.title: Arcanum Data
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum data.

So the last few weeks — well, since I’ve I I posted this almost a year ago, I think — since I converted Arcanum to be a Windows, a modern Windows app, it’s way easier to debug it I can put break points anywhere. I can run the game in a window, you know, hit the break points wherever I want, look at what is going on, because you know it’s been 25 years more since code started on that game. I don’t remember how everything works and I didn’t write everything. There were three other programmers, so I’ve been going through it for various reasons, and one of them I was doing recently was I was trying to see how the data flow was handled, basically what was loaded when in the game. And first of all, let me just say there is a lot of data in this game. I look at it and I have to remind myself that the entire game was made by essentially 14 people. Yes, there’s a lot more people in the credits, but you know I’m not counting people who did localization and production and you know, uh made the boxes and handled advertisements and things like that. I’m just talking about the actual development team. It was 14 people.

item.title: Arcanum Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s walk through the Arcanum development timeline.

Few weeks ago — actually may even be a couple months ago — I did the Fallout development timeline. People really like that. In fact, stavos Nicola asked: can you do an Arcanum one as well? And I was like, yes, um, interestingly about doing this. So this timeline was a lot harder for me to put together than the Fallout. One was basically looking at my notes and ordering them chronologically.

item.title: Thoughts On An Arcanum Remake
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about thoughts on an Arcanum remake.

Uh, I’ve had different questions about it I even touched upon this a little bit in the video I did on Arcanum as a Windows 10 app, where I actually have Arcanum up and running in Windows 10, and I talked about some things I might do about it, but I didn’t finish the thought. I didn’t do it as completely as I did say in the video called thoughts on a Fallout remake. So I’ll link those two videos below, but I want to do this one, which it will kind of collect all my thoughts on.

item.title: Arcanum As A Windows 10 App
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum.

I have talked about it before, but people often say: what do you spend your time doing? And I’m, like I’ve mentioned, I work for a couple clients, but I also love messing around with games as toys, and one of the toy games I mess around with is Arcanum, and I did that about. I want to say it was about six months ago. This is November of 2023.

item.title: Arcanum Dialogs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I’m going to walk you through an Arcanum dialogue file explains how everything works and, along the way, kind of explain why we did it this way.

So I’m going to put the file up, boom. So this is a the actual example NPC dialog file that I made back when we were making Arcanum, to show people who would want to make dialogues for the game, kind of how it worked. So let me walk through how this works.

item.title: Arcanum Press Event
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about arcanum’s press event.

This was a really fun press event that was called editor’s day that we held in April of 2000. Sierra, the publisher, invited press to preview Arcanum at the Magic Castle, which is a club in Los Angeles. It’s a really cool Club. It is only open to magicians and their guests. Professional magicians and some pretty famous people have appeared at the magic castle.

item.title: Arcanum Tech Skills & Schematics
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum Tech skills and schematics, and the reason I want to talk about that is

I’ve had enough videos where I’ve talked about designs, changing other people having better design ideas than me, the whole process of doing a game from start to finish with pillars, design pillars and so many other considerations that the reason I want to talk about Arcanum Tech skills is. I want to talk about something you probably haven’t heard about, which is they started completely differently than how they ended up. I was going through my Troika file archive, which is really big, and I found the original master design dock for Arcanum. That was written in early 90s, late 98, early 99, and I found that originally there were a lot more Tech skills than what we planned. So let me walk you through. What happened originally.

item.title: Arcanum Scripting
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about scripting in Arcanum.

Now I was going to compare and contrast it with Fallout scripting. But Fallout script came from directly from Starfleet Academy. I never used it I wasn’t the one who integrated it in. So while I know what it was like, I don’t really have any use in it, and so I’m gonna defer that for now.

item.title: Arcanum AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to answer some questions that have come up about how the AI worked in Arcanum.

Before I start, though, I should think it’s funny. I just got a Google or YouTube analytics email, because I’ve been doing this for more than 90 days, where it was telling me what videos were popular and what weren’t, to try to give me advice of what to do and the advice seems to be skewed towards doing things that get more people to watch ads, because it told me these technical ones — well, those attracted less viewers and, according to YouTube, they don’t think people want that. Which ones do they want? Why I quit Fallout 2? Why I quit WildStar?

item.title: Arcanum Random Encounters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s Random Encounters.

This is a not a follow-up but a parallel to the video I did on Fallout Random Encounters. A few people asked me how Arcanum worked and how different it was and how we handled certain special Random Encounters that happened at a particular time of the game. I would admit that when the when these questions came in, all I could remember is it had something to do with arcanums timed event system. We had a really complicated timed event system, which is basically a queue of events that any system in the game could throw things into, and they could be either real time, which is based on the actual time in going on, or it could be game time. In fact, there was a second timer called game time two. One of those was affected by pauses in the game and the other one wasn’t um.

item.title: Arcanum Generated Dialog
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s generated dialogue system, and I know

I’ve been talking about Arcanum a lot, but I mean I’ve got the code right in front of me. I’ve been going through it a lot, I’ve been playing with it, and the generated dialogue system is something I’m particularly proud of because it let the few designers that were on arcanuman — we had even of the fewer designers, we had even fewer narrative designers — and it let those few people I mean think about. We only had 14 people Tops on Arcanum and just a few writing dialogue. They let them make so much content in the game, which so it was a good tool. But in particular, I liked it because it really doubled down on arcanum’s reactivity, and I’ll talk about why, um, when I get to the NPC one. But this could be really long, I’m probably talking really fast, but there’s just so much to talk about and it’s a detailed topic for me.

item.title: Arcanum Prototypes & Structures
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prototypes and structures in Arcanum.

I did the previous talk on how we did procedural generation in Arcanum and now I want to talk about how we actually stored objects. Uh, that was a question several people had and then related to the procedural generation. I mentioned that, uh, buildings were done a little differently, so I’ll explain that too. So before I can explain how prototypes work, I kind of have to explain how objects work and even why we did prototypes, which was basically to save memory.

item.title: Arcanum's Procedural Terrain Generation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to talk about arcanum’s procedural terrain generation system, gotten some questions about this for years.

The fact that we did this in the late 90s I thought was pretty cool. It also was pretty required because there was no way. No way we could store as much map, especially Overland map, as we wanted in the computers of the time. We just didn’t have the memory, so, basically, the procedural generation was a way for us to generate maps and treat what we edited as Deltas to those Maps, and I will explain all this. But I went through the code to make sure I was accurate.

item.title: Arcanum Combat: Real Time vs Turn Based
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanums, various combat modes, the real time, turn-based and fast turn based, kind of what the ideas were behind that, why it’s even in the game, um, what I wish

I had done differently. So go back to 1998. We had originally specced out Arcanum before we talked to Sierra.

Um. We had a rough outline of what we wanted to do and we were planning to make it turn based. We wanted to do turn based. We coming off Fallout, we understood turn based, turn-based RPGs have something that I really like, which is, as I’ve said in another video, when I cast a spell, especially a spell that has an area of effect, and especially in an isometric game, when things are moving around while I’m casting it, that drives me insane. I mean I liked Baldur’s Gate, but that it drove me insane to try to cast like a fireball in that spell.

item.title: Arcanum: Explaining The Void
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the void in Arcanum and this is something that somebody asked about.

Somebody asked if I could talk about the void because it was mysterious and they wouldn’t know the back story and how we came up with it and if there were any plans to use it in future games, so I know that the void came up um as an idea in Arcanum because it kind of filled the void of answering questions for two different things. The first one was when we made the list of magic spells, there was one that lets you speak with dead. We originally envisioned it as how speak with dad works in AD or DND version one, whatever you call that — where you could ask a set number of questions and the higher level you were, the more questions you could ask.

item.title: Arcanum Criticals
item.description:
Hi everyone, this is Tim. Today I want to talk about how criticals work in Arcanum.

Sounds like a simple question. I completely went down the rabbit hole in arcanum’s code. I think I mentioned once that Arcanum was very much our everything in the kitchen sink game, and I think every single idea that anyone ever had about criticals — whether it was combat or not, whether it was success or failure — went into Arcanum. So if this is something that interests you keep listening, because I have an awful lot of stuff to go over.

item.title: Arcanum Attribute Effects
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to go over the effects of the attributes in Arcanum.

Yesterday I talked about the effects of the special attributes in Fallout. People really seem to like that, so I did. I’m going to go through our Canon today. Let me address: yes, my shirt is super wrinkly. I got this at the very bottom of the box I found in the garage. It is when we shipped Arcanum and yet this is an Arcanum shirt.

item.title: Arcanum's Original Attribute System
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Arcanum attribute system and specifically the original attributes, the way they were designed, not the ones we shipped with, and I’m answering a question.

Someone asked — I’m sorry about this. Like over a month ago, maybe almost six weeks ago, somebody asked what the how I came up with the beauty stat for Arcanum which made me look at the design dock, which made me look at the original design dock, which made me go whoa, I was planning to do something entirely different, so let me talk about that. So, brief refresher: if you remember the attributes for Arcanum, there were eight of them. They were physical and mental, and then there were these four categories called Power, resistant, resistance, prowess, which I’ve never liked an appearance. So the physical ones were strength, con, Dex and Beauty, and the mental ones were intelligence, will, power, perception and charisma.

item.title: The Arcanum One-Point Fiasco
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what I now call, in very distant hindsight, the Arcanum.

One point, Fiasco, but let me give you context. I love to give context, so recently I had someone who was a director — director - director at a company — ask me how I made decisions as a director. Um, they were often asked to make decisions on things that they weren’t quite sure about. Either it wasn’t in their area of expertise or they just weren’t sure. There were multiple Solutions and they weren’t sure. And at the end of this video I’ll tell you what I told them, not because I don’t want to tell you now, but because you need to understand big things that happened to me to understand why I gave this person the answer I did now. I talked about in The Fallout video that there were decisions made that didn’t 100 agree with.

item.title: Arcanum 2 System Extensions
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to talk about Arcanum 2..

Specifically the system mechanics extensions that I designed for it, because I was reading through them recently and I’m like, hey, these are pretty cool. I kind of wish we had done this. Just want to point out before I begin I do not own the rights to Arcanum. Those are owned by Activision Blizzard, so there’s no need to ask if I’m working on an Arcanum 2 or can make an Arcanum to. I can’t ask Activision Blizzard if you have any questions. So I was going through my design folder from Troika and I found one that was literally called Arcanum 2 system extensions.

item.title: A Chat With Leonard Boyarsky, Part 2
item.description:
Him and I’m joined again today for part two of my chat with Leonard bararski. Hey Leonard, hey Tim, how’s it going? I’m good.

Before we get started, I saw that you, uh, showed your gnod trophy. I wanted to show my Award of Excellence that interplay gave me. Um, the funny thing about this is I think Jason got one too. There was a party — there’s a party — where all of interplay was at this party and they gave out that award. I don’t know if this is what happened. I think this is what happened with Gana too. You don’t remember what I’m gonna say, do you? So the next day, Rob comes in and he gives it to me. I’m like what is this?

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

The channel has now passed 15 000 subscribers. I’m still reading all the comments, but I have given up on replying to any of them. Um, I used to reply to some occasionally.

Part of it is there’s just too many coming in too fast, literally why? Why I was answering a question, another question would come in also. I’ve noticed that even when I’ve answered questions, people then ask the same question, sometimes in the same section. So what I decided to do is I gathered up a lot of questions have been answered or been asked multiple times and I’m going to answer them here. This is going to be my first fact. My first frequent answer asked questions.

item.title: Arcanum 2: Journey to the Center of Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum.

So let me give you some background. Um, for those of you new, Troika games was a game Studio that I started with Leonard barski and Jason Anderson on April 1st 1998, and our first game that we shipped was Arcanum. Arcanum shipped in 2001 using a new isometric engine we made. However, even before it shipped, we started thinking about ideas for sequels and that we wanted to make the jump to 3D. And we were talking to valve, because the VP of valve, Scott Lynch, had been the VP who signed us to Sierra for Arcanum and we were talking with them about their Source engine and we really wanted to use it. So we put together a design dock, which I still have. I read through and I’m like: Whoa, We were very ambitious. Uh, Troika liked to do a lot of things. In fact, Arcana might commonly refer to as our kitchen.
